query: US Iran nuclear negotiations July 2026 direct face-to-face talks vs indirect mediated format latest status
answer:
I've completed my research on the US-Iran nuclear negotiations format status. Here's the summary of key findings:

## Key Findings

**Current format: INDIRECT.** As of July 12-13, 2026, US-Iran talks remain indirect, conducted through Qatari, Pakistani, and Omani mediators — the two sides do not meet in the same room in the current technical track.

**Iran's stance:** Tehran has consistently and explicitly refused direct talks. Iran's foreign ministry stated it had "no plans for negotiations with the American side at any level," and repeatedly rejected US claims that talks were "direct." VP Vance dismissed Iran's denials as "a Persian negotiating tactic."

**On the July 11-23 window specifically:**
- Multiple sources (Anadolu July 2; Dawn/Independent Pakistan July 5) reported that officials *expected* a round of high-level **DIRECT** negotiations in Doha during the "third week of July" (~July 13-19), after technical teams worked through details.
- Technical talks were expected to resume ~July 11, with Islamabad as the front-runner venue.

**The critical complication:** A major military escalation (July 7-13) derailed the diplomatic track:
- Trump declared the ceasefire "OVER" on July 8
- US conducted 300+ strikes over three nights (July 9-12)
- On July 12, Iran attacked five Gulf states — including mediators Qatar and Oman — declared the Strait of Hormuz closed, and **denied even requesting US talks**
- The only near-term direct-contact mechanism reported was a mediated multi-party **phone call**, not an in-person meeting

**Precedent for direct talks exists** (Islamabad Talks had 2 of 3 rounds direct; Lucerne Summit involved "some direct talks"), but the escalation and Iran's hardening posture make an imminent shift to same-room direct talks highly uncertain and currently derailed.

The full structured findings — including key facts, sources, and quantitative data points — have been submitted via the StructuredOutput tool.

key_facts: As of July 12-13, 2026, US-Iran talks remain INDIRECT, conducted via Qatari, Pakistani, and Omani mediators; the two sides do not meet in the same room in the current technical track.

Iran has repeatedly and explicitly DENIED holding direct talks; FM spokesman Baqaei said Iran had 'no plans for negotiations with the American side at any level,' and rejected Trump's 'direct' characterization.
US officials (VP Vance) claimed direct technical talks were occurring and dismissed Iran's denials as 'a Persian negotiating tactic.'

Sources (Anadolu Jul 2; Dawn/Independent Pakistan Jul 5) reported officials EXPECTED a round of high-level DIRECT negotiations in Doha during the 'third week of July' (~Jul 13-19), overlapping the Jul 11-23 window.
Technical talks were expected to resume ~July 11, with Islamabad the front-runner venue over Bürgenstock, Switzerland.

A major escalation began July 7-8: Iran attacked Hormuz shipping, US struck ~80 then ~140 targets (300+ over three nights), and Trump declared the ceasefire 'OVER' on July 8 (while leaving door open to talks).
On July 12, Iran attacked five Gulf states including mediators Qatar and Oman for the first time in months, declared the Strait of Hormuz closed, and DENIED requesting US talks.
The only near-term direct-contact mechanism reported was a mediated multi-party PHONE call (US-Iran-Qatar-Pakistan) being arranged around July 11-12 — not an in-person same-room meeting.

Historical precedent exists for some direct contact: Islamabad Talks (Apr 11-12) had 2 of 3 rounds direct; Lake Lucerne/Bürgenstock Summit (Jun 21-22) involved 'some direct talks' in a quadrilateral format.
The nuclear program itself was explicitly deferred to later talks under the 60-day MoU window (signed June 17, 2026); July talks focused on Hormuz and frozen assets ($6bn), not nuclear specifics.
Iran's chief negotiator Qalibaf posted on July 12: 'The era of one-sided deals is OVER,' and Iran said it will not negotiate while under US attack — signaling hardening on format.
